title: Capacity-constrained decoder attention/KV memory and NoC baseline
hypothesis: Once local/shared SRAM placements are constrained by practical die-area-derived capacity, the optimal attention/KV memory placement will move from HBM or spill-heavy shared SRAM on small dies toward local/shared SRAM only at sufficiently large SRAM budgets.

direct_comparison.primary_question: How does the best feasible attention/KV memory placement move as die area, SRAM fraction, usable SRAM, density, banking, NoC bandwidth, and hop count vary?
include: MQA, GQA8, and MHA at 8-bit and 16-bit KV

expected_benefit: prevents infeasible local-SRAM latency winners from dominating architecture selection
shows which die-size range can practically hold 131k KV cache on chip
selects the next integrated memory/NoC measurement point
risks: NoC scheduling is approximated as bandwidth divided by hop count
bank conflicts and arbitration are not modeled
SRAM capacity uses area proxy rather than generated macro data

required_evaluations.objective: Run a capacity-constrained attention/KV memory and NoC baseline sweep across die size and SRAM assumptions.

expected_result.reason: Use the capacity-feasible best points to choose the next integrated attention/KV SRAM and NoC scheduling measurement.
